title: Throwing a teapot lid in stoneware
subtitle: How to throw a flanged lid that drops into a thrown teapot gallery
excerpt: A step-by-step technique for throwing a flanged lid to fit a thrown teapot: measuring the gallery opening with calipers, throwing the lid dome upside-down on the wheel, forming a flange that drops precisely into the gallery. Cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Requires a potter's wheel and kiln.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage at which clay holds its shape but remains damp enough to trim and join
A narrow horizontal ledge thrown or trimmed inside the rim of a lidded vessel on which the lid flange rests
The first kiln firing (cone 06, approx 1000 degrees C) that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware
Pyrometric measure of heat-work in a kiln. Cone 06 approx 1000 degrees C, cone 6 approx 1220 degrees C, cone 10 approx 1280 degrees C
Fully air-dried unfired clay; fragile before bisque firing

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no dry sanding indoors.

Ensure adequate ventilation when firing: a downdraft vent or open window with an extractor. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Do not open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach.
A flanged teapot lid is thrown upside-down: the dome forms at the bottom of the piece, and the flange that drops into the
gallery

sits at the outer rim. Throwing upside-down gives better control over the flange diameter because the caliper measurement applies directly to the outer edge while the clay is still soft. The lid is inverted onto the banding wheel at
leather-hard
stage to add the knob. The fit holds through firing shrinkage of
bisque
and glaze firing to
cone
6 (1220 degrees C) if measurements are taken and applied accurately.
Preparation: measuring the teapot body

Before throwing the lid, measure the gallery inside the teapot body with the calipers. Open the calipers to span the inside diameter of the gallery opening at the point where the lid flange will sit. Record this measurement: this is the target flange diameter. The caliper spans the inside of the gallery because it is the inside of the gallery that determines how wide the flange must be to drop in cleanly without wobbling. Subtract approximately 2 mm from the measurement to account for the fit tolerance needed at the wet throwing stage (the flange will shrink slightly and tighten to the gallery as both pieces dry and fire together).
Throwing the lid upside-down
Centre 200 g of smooth stoneware on the wheel. The wedging stage for lid clay is the same as for the body: thorough, with no air pockets.
Flatten the centred ball into a low dome approximately 80 mm in diameter and 30 mm tall. This dome will become the inside curve of the lid when it is inverted after firing.
Open from the centre of the dome downward to within 6 mm of the bat or wheel head. Pull the opening outward to create a shallow bowl shape approximately 50 mm in interior diameter.

At the outermost rim of the thrown piece, create the flange ring: use a finger to press inward and slightly downward at the outer edge, forming a step approximately 4 mm wide. This stepped-down rim is the flange that will sit inside the teapot gallery. The outer diameter of this flange ring is the measurement you recorded from the calipers, minus the 2 mm tolerance.

Check the flange diameter immediately with the calipers: set them to the adjusted target measurement and check the flange ring fits between the caliper arms with light friction, not a tight grip. Adjust if necessary by pressing the flange ring inward or opening it slightly.
Smooth the interior of the dome (which will be the visible upper surface of the lid) with the wooden rib. Use the chamois leather to refine the rim where the flange meets the dome.

Wire the lid from the wheel head and leave it upside-down on a small board to dry to leather-hard. This is how it will be handled for knob addition and trimming: upside-down is the working position throughout the wet stage.
Adding the knob
At
leather-hard
stage, invert the lid so it now sits right-way-up on the banding wheel. The flat dome is now uppermost. Centre it by eye on the banding wheel.
Prepare a small ball of clay approximately 15 g for the knob. Score and slip the centre of the lid dome and the base of the knob clay. Press the knob ball firmly onto the centre of the lid dome.

Shape the knob by pulling and pinching it upward to a height of approximately 18 to 20 mm, finishing in a rounded or slightly flattened top. The knob must be thick enough at its base to resist pulling off in use: a base diameter of at least 18 mm is recommended. Blend the base of the knob into the lid surface with a damp fingertip.
Re-check the flange diameter with the calipers after the knob is attached: the lid should not have been squeezed or distorted during knob attachment. Adjust if needed.
Trimming the flange

When the lid with knob has stiffened again to leather-hard, invert it one more time on the banding wheel so the flange ring is accessible. Use the loop tool to trim the underside of the flange precisely: the flange should be 4 mm wide, flat on its underside, and its vertical depth (the drop into the gallery) should be 5 to 6 mm. A deeper flange seats more securely and prevents the lid rattling when the teapot is poured.

Check the fit against the actual teapot body while both pieces are still at leather-hard. Lower the lid onto the teapot gallery. It should drop in with slight resistance and sit level. If the flange is too large and will not drop in, trim it slightly with the loop tool. If it drops in too loosely, the fit cannot be corrected at this stage: start a new lid.
Allow both the lid and the teapot body to dry to
bone-dry
together on a level surface before bisque firing.
Firing with the lid in place

Bisque fire the lid resting in the gallery of the teapot body. Both pieces shrink by approximately the same percentage during firing, so the fit is maintained. Apply a light coat of alumina wash to the gallery ledge before the glaze firing to prevent the lid flange fusing to the body at glaze temperature. Fire to cone 6 (1220 degrees C).
Why this technique works

Throwing upside-down places the flange at the outermost point where the caliper can measure it directly. Right-way-up throwing places the flange on an inward-projecting shelf, which is much harder to measure accurately while the piece is on the wheel.
The knob is added at leather-hard, not thrown with the dome while wet, because wet clay distorts under pressure and makes caliper checks unreliable.

Checking the live fit at leather-hard, when both the lid and the body are still adjustable, is the only reliable way to guarantee the fired fit. Every 2 mm tolerance error at the wet stage becomes proportionally larger after firing shrinkage.

rows.problem: Lid does not fit the gallery after bisque firing
rows.cause: Flange diameter was not measured against the live gallery before firing, or the caliper measurement was taken at the wrong position inside the gallery
rows.fix: Always check the leather-hard fit directly. After bisque, a slightly oversized flange can sometimes be adjusted by wrapping fine wet-dry paper around a cylindrical form and grinding the flange edge lightly.

rows.problem: Knob cracked at the join after drying
rows.cause: The knob clay was attached when the lid dome was too dry, or the score and slip was insufficient
rows.fix: Score and slip both surfaces thoroughly. Re-wet the lid dome surface before attaching the knob if the dome has already passed soft leather-hard.

rows.cause: Flange diameter is slightly too small for the gallery
rows.fix: A small gap can be acceptable in a functional teapot. If the fit is very loose, the lid can be set in the gallery with a thin bead of food-safe silicon sealant on the flange as a non-permanent fix.
